q: A disease is an illness or disorder of the body or mind that leads to poor health.
Non-infectious diseases are those that are not caused by pathogens and cannot spread from one person to another.
Examples of non-infectious diseases include cardiovascular disease, cancer, chronic respiratory disease, diabetes, and Alzheimer’s.
Most cardiovascular disease starts with atheroma formation.
An atheroma is a fibrous plaque formed when white blood cells (mostly macrophages), lipids and connective tissue build up and harden.
The hardening of arteries, caused by atheroma, is called atherosclerosis.
In angina, plaques build up slowly in the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the parts of the heart muscle beyond the plaques.
In angina, the narrowed coronary arteries cannot supply enough oxygenated blood and the heart muscle resorts to anaerobic respiration.
The symptoms of angina subside once exercise stops.
Myocardial infarction is more commonly known as a stroke.
Myocardial infarction occurs when a coronary artery becomes completely blocked by a blood clot, cutting off an area of the heart muscle from its blood supply.
A stroke is caused by an interruption to the normal blood supply to an area of the brain.
Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is the formation of a blood clot in a vein deep inside the body, usually in leg veins.
Genes, age, and gender are non-modifiable risk factors for cardiovascular disease.
As a person gets older, the blood vessels begin to lose their elasticity and to narrow slightly.
Men are more likely to suffer from heart disease and other CVDs than women.
Lifestyle factors such as smoking, diet, and inactivity are non-modifiable risk factors for CVD.
Substances in tobacco smoke can damage the artery linings and cause the arteries to narrow.
Sedentary lifestyle can lead to heart diseases and also causes obesity, high blood pressure and diabetes.
A healthy blood pressure is around $120 mmHg$ during systole and around $80 mmHg$ during diastole.
Blood pressure regularly above $140/90 mmHg$ is a sign of hypertension and can be a sign of atherosclerosis.
An increasing number of studies suggest that being overweight directly affects the risk of developing CVDs.
A body mass index (BMI) of $18.5-25 kgm^{-2}$ is the ideal range.
Antihypertensives drugs such as beta blockers, sympathetic nerve inhibitors, and ACE inhibitors are used to reduce blood pressure.
Statins are a group of drugs used to lower the level of cholesterol in the blood.
Plant stanols and sterols compounds are now widely sold in spreads and yoghurts to reduce the levels of LDLs in the blood.
Anticoagulants, such as warfarin and heparin, are a class of medicines used to increase blood clotting.
Platelet inhibitory drugs such as aspirin are a type of anticoagulant that prevent platelets clumping together.
Infectious diseases are diseases that are caused by organisms known as pathogens.
The prevalence of a disease is the number of people who have that disease at any one time.
Diseases that are always in populations are described as endemic.
An epidemic occurs when there is a sudden increase in the number of people with a disease.
A pandemic occurs when there is an increase in the number of cases throughout a continent or across the world.
The death rate from different diseases is referred to as mortality.
Malaria is caused by four species of the protist Plasmodium.
The insect vector for malaria is the female Anopheles mosquito.
The site of action of the malaria pathogen includes the liver, red blood cells, and brain.
Female Anopheles mosquitoes feed on human blood to obtain the protein they need to develop their eggs.
Male and female gametes of Plasmodium fuse in the human liver.
Plasmodium can pass across the placenta from mother to fetus.
Plasmodium multiplies in both hosts, the human and the mosquito.
Immunity to malaria only lasts as long as people are in contact with the disease.
Quinine and chloroquine are anti-malarial drugs used to treat infected people.
Chloroquine inhibits protein synthesis and prevents the parasite spreading within the body.
Proguanil has the added advantage of inhibiting the sexual reproduction of Plasmodium inside the biting mosquito.
Artesunate is a drug derived from the plant compound artemisinin.
Gambusia affinis is a fish that feeds on mosquito larvae.
The bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis kills mosquito larvae and is highly toxic to other forms of life.
One reason for the failure of the WHO malaria eradication programme was that Plasmodium became resistant to the drugs used to control it.
Mosquitoes became resistant to DDT (dichloro-diphenyl-trichloroethane).
